passage_title: The International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI) 2026 task submission notice
passage_text:
The International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI) 2026 Scientific Committee invites the IOI community to help design the competition tasks. IOI tasks focus on efficient, correct algorithms (算法) with simple input and output. IOI needs creative tasks that require insight and allow multiple approaches. The nature of past tasks should not restrict the design of new tasks. We are particularly interested in tasks with accessible basic rules and tasks that illustrate algorithms arising from real-life situations.
Requirements
·Be secret to potential IOI 2026 contestants
·Not used in any recent similar competition
·Solvable during an IOI round
·Clear, easy-to-understand statements
·Original and/ or innovative
Task Criteria
·Statement in English
·Description of the full-score algorithm
·Author contact and background: Name, email, country, and roles in IOI or national Olympiad (IOI 2024-2026)
Submission Process & Method
·Pack all materials into a single file (.zip or .tgz for multiple files) and submit online.
·Authorization Code: samarkand
·Encryption (加密): do not encrypt on your own; the upload page handles it automatically.
What Happens Next
A shortlist of around 10 tasks will be selected, six of which are expected to be used at IOI 2026 in Uzbekistan.
Authors of shortlisted tasks may be invited to attend IOI 2026 as guests (travel typically at the author’s expense; accommodations covered by the host).
Authors of tasks included in the official competition will be credited on the IOI website.
Deadline: March 31, 2026, 23:59 UTC+5
Contact Information: hsc@ioi2026.uz

questions.stem: What type of tasks is given special priority to by the committee?

options.label: A
options.text: Tasks reflecting real-life applications.

options.label: B
options.text: Original tasks with complex descriptions.

options.label: C
options.text: Highly demanding tasks for top participants.

options.label: D
options.text: Tasks closely following past competition patterns.

questions.explanation: QA support states that the committee is particularly interested in tasks with accessible basic rules and algorithms arising from real-life situations.

passage_title: Four Famous Horse Racing Jockeys (骑师) in the World
passage_text:
Four Famous Horse Racing Jockeys (骑师) in the World
Ruby Walsh
Ruby Walsh, whose father was an Irish jockey, showed early promise. He won the Irish amateur title twice before going professional. At 20, he made history by winning the English Grand National on his first attempt in 2000. Walsh had a really successful career with lots of praise and honours. He became the Irish Jump Jockey champion twelve times. Between 2004 and 2017, he dominated the Cheltenham Festival with 59 wins, the most ever, and claimed the leading rider’s award 11 times.
Laffit Pincay
Laffit Pincay, from Panama, was brought to the US by Fred W. Hooper in 1966. Starting at Arlington Park, he won eight of his first eleven races, quickly gaining nationwide recognition. He won almost every major US race, including two American classic races. He was the US Champion Jockey by Earnings 7 times and by Wins once, and was admitted into the National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame in 1975.
Lester Piggott
Lester Piggott, an English jockey and trainer, started racing at 12 and won his first race in 1948 at Haydock Park. He became the youngest to ride and win over 100 races in a season. He won numerous top UK races, with records like 9 Epsom Derby wins and 11 Ascot Gold Cup wins.
Russell Baze
Coming from a horse-racing family, Russell Baze began his career at 16 at the Yakima racetrack in Washington, winning his first race in 1974. He became a major force in American racing, winning the United States Champion Jockey by Wins 10 times. At Bay Meadows, he won 36 titles, and at Golden Gate Fields, 27.
